tree.heading: Overview
tree.content_preview: This guideline covers renal replacement therapy (dialysis and transplantation) and conservative management for people with chronic kidney disease stages 4 and 5. It aims to improve quality of life by

tree.heading: Who is it for?
tree.content_preview:
Healthcare professionals
Providers of renal replacement therapy and conservative management
People with chronic kidney disease stages 4 and 5, their families and carers

tree.heading: Recommendations
tree.content_preview:
People have the right to be involved in discussions and make informed decisions about their care, as described in NICE's information on making decisions about your care.

Making decisions using NICE g

children.heading: 1.1 Indications for starting dialysis
children.content_preview:
1.1.1 Follow the recommendations on referral criteria in NICE's guideline on chronic kidney disease.
1.1.2 Consider starting dialysis when indicated by the impact of symptoms of uraemia on daily livin

children.heading: 1.2 Preparing for renal replacement therapy or conservative management

tree.heading: When to assess
tree.content_preview:
1.2.1 Start assessment for renal replacement therapy (RRT) or conservative management at least 1 year before therapy is likely to be needed, including for those with a failing transplant.

For a short

tree.heading: How to assess
tree.content_preview:

clinical preparation
psychosocial evaluation, preparati

children.heading: 1.3 Choosing modalities of renal replacement therapy or conservative management
children.content_preview: 1.3.1 Offer a choice of RRT or conservative management to people who are likely to need RRT. Conservative management for children should only be considered within appropriate regulatory frameworks. Se

children.heading: 1.3.3 Offer people (and their family members or carers, as appropriate) regular opportunities:
children.content_preview:
to review the decision regarding RRT modalities or conservative management
to discuss any concerns or changes in their preferences.

tree.heading: Transplantation
tree.content_preview:
1.3.4 Discuss the individual factors that affect the risks and benefits of transplantation with all people who are likely to need RRT, and their family members or carers (as appropriate).

tree.content_preview:
1.3.8 Offer a choice of dialysis modalities at home or in centre ensuring that the decision is informed by clinical considerations and patient preferences (see recommendation 1.3.2).

children.heading: 1.4 Planning dialysis access formation
children.content_preview: 1.4.1 Discuss with the person, their family members and carers (as appropriate) the risk and benefits of the different types of dialysis access, for example, fistula, graft, central venous or peritone

children.heading: 1.5 Indications for switching or stopping renal replacement therapy
children.content_preview:
1.5.1 Offer information on all medically appropriate treatment options when discussing switching RRT modality.
1.5.2 Consider switching treatment modality or stopping RRT if medically indicated or if

children.heading: 1.6 Recognising symptoms
children.content_preview: 1.6.1 Recognise that people on RRT or receiving conservative management may have the symptoms in box 1 and that these may affect their day-to-day life.

tree.heading: Box 1 Possible symptoms in people on renal replacement therapy or conservative management
tree.content_preview:
General symptoms:
breathlessness
fatigue
insomnia
itching
lethargy
pain
poor appetite
swelling
taste changes
thirst
weakness
weight loss/gain.

Gastro-intestinal/urological symptoms:
abdominal cramps

children.heading: 1.6.2 Throughout the course of RRT and conservative management:
children.content_preview:
Ask people about any symptoms they have.
Explore whether symptoms are due to the renal condition, treatment or another cause.
Explain the likely cause of the symptoms and how well treatment may be exp
ancestor_path: Box 1 Possible symptoms in people on renal replacement therapy or conservative management

children.heading: 1.7 Diet and fluids
children.content_preview:
1.7.1 Offer a full dietary assessment by a specialist renal dietitian to people starting dialysis or conservative management. This should include:
weight history
fluid intake
sodium
potassium
phosphat
ancestor_path: Box 1 Possible symptoms in people on renal replacement therapy or conservative management

children.heading: 1.8 Information, education and support
children.content_preview:
1.8.1 To enable people, and their families and carers (as appropriate), to make informed decisions, offer balanced and accurate information about:
all treatments available to them (including RRT modal
ancestor_path: Box 1 Possible symptoms in people on renal replacement therapy or conservative management

tree.heading: Box 2 Information about treatments and how they may affect lifestyle
tree.content_preview:
Information about treatments, including RRT, conservative management and dietary intervention:
What they involve, for example, availability of assistance, time that treatment takes place, and number o

children.content_preview:

before they start RRT or conservative management, and
when they switch from o

children.content_preview:
delivering an approach to care that takes account of multimorbidity in NICE's guideline on multimorbidity, and
continuity of care and relationships, and enabling patients to actively participate in th

tree.heading: Recommendations for research
tree.content_preview: The guideline committee has made the following recommendations for research .

tree.heading: Key recommendations for research
1 Cardiac assessment
tree.content_preview:
What is the clinical and cost effectiveness of cardiac assessment before transplantation?

For a short explanation of why the committee made the recommendation for research, see the rationale section

tree.heading: 2 Timing of pre-emptive transplant
tree.content_preview:
What is the most clinical and cost-effective strategy for timing of pre-emptive transplantation?

For a short explanation of why the committee made the recommendation for research, see the rationale s

tree.heading: 3 Acute haemodialysis versus acute peritoneal dialysis
tree.content_preview:
What is the clinical and cost effectiveness of initial haemodialysis versus initial peritoneal dialysis for people who start dialysis in an unplanned way?

For a short explanation of why the committee

tree.heading: 4 Frequency of review
tree.content_preview:
What is the most clinical and cost-effective frequency of review for people on peritoneal dialysis, haemodiafiltration, haemodialysis or conservative management?

For a short explanation of why the co

tree.heading: 5 Coordinating care
tree.content_preview:
What is the clinical and cost effectiveness of having keyworkers present in the context of renal replacement therapy (RRT)?

For a short explanation of why the committee made the recommendation for re

tree.heading: Other recommendations for research
6 Switching RRT modality
tree.content_preview: What is the clinical and cost effectiveness of strategies for switching RRT modality?

tree.heading: 7 Decision aids for RRT
tree.content_preview:
What is the clinical and cost effectiveness of using decision aids in the context of RRT?

For a short explanation of why the committee made the recommendation for research, see the rationale section

tree.heading: 8 Laparoscopic and percutaneous peritoneal dialysis access creation
tree.content_preview: What is the optimum timing of laparoscopic and percutaneous peritoneal dialysis access creation?

tree.heading: 9 Conservative management
tree.content_preview: What is the clinical and cost effectiveness of conservative management versus dialysis in frail, older people?

tree.heading: 10 Home haemodiafiltration
tree.content_preview: What is the clinical and cost effectiveness of home haemodiafiltration versus home haemodialysis, taking into account the impact of frequency?

tree.heading: 11 haemodialysis/haemodiafiltration before peritoneal dialysis
tree.content_preview:
What is the clinical and cost effectiveness of haemodialysis/haemodiafiltration before

peritoneal dialysis versus peritoneal dialysis before haemodialysis/haemodiafiltration?

tree.heading: 12 Listing for transplantation
tree.content_preview: What is the optimum timing of listing for transplantation?

tree.heading: How the recommendations might affect practice
tree.content_preview: The recommendations reflect common practice for adults and children, and so are not likely to involve a change of practice for most NHS providers or have a substantial resource impact for the NHS in E

tree.heading: Preparing for renal replacement therapy or conservative management - when to assess
tree.content_preview: Recommendation 1.2.1

tree.heading: Why the committee made the recommendation
tree.content_preview: Some evidence indicated that earlier referral to nephrology services improved survival on RRT at 90 days. The committee were interested in the timing of referral for assessment for RRT and used their

tree.heading: How the recommendation might affect practice
tree.content_preview:
The recommendation generally reflects current practice so there should be no significant change in practice or substantial resource impact to the NHS in England.

tree.content_preview: The committee recognised that an assessment should involve preparing people for RRT, for example, by explaining the procedures to create vascular access and checking heart function and immunity. Psych

tree.heading: How the recommendations might affect practice
tree.content_preview: Psychological assessment in people at high risk of non-adherence or morbidity is current practice in many areas. The recommendation is likely to lead to better targeting of psychological assessment in

tree.heading: Choosing modalities of renal replacement therapy or conservative management
tree.content_preview: Recommendations 1.3.1 to 1.3.11

tree.heading: Why the committee made the recommendations
Renal replacement therapy or conservative management
tree.content_preview: People who are likely to need RRT should be supported to make decisions about treatment options, including conservative management. There was no evidence of differential benefits or harms in any speci

tree.heading: Choice of renal replacement therapy
tree.content_preview: Evidence showed that if RRT is chosen, transplantation offers a clear advantage over dialysis in terms of extending life. This benefit was observed in each age group. In the committee's experience, qu

tree.heading: Sequencing
tree.content_preview: There was not enough evidence to recommend any particular sequence of RRT modalities. The committee agreed that decisions about sequence would mostly be guided by personal circumstances.

tree.heading: How the recommendations might affect practice
tree.content_preview: Currently there is a mix of HDF and HD provision in the NHS in England. The recommendation to consider HDF over HD for in-centre dialysis may affect resource use in areas where this is a change in pra

tree.heading: Why the committee made the recommendations
tree.content_preview:
The committee highlighted the importance of discussing with the person the different types of dialysis and their access and the impacts of these on everyday life.

tree.heading: How the recommendations might affect practice
tree.content_preview:
Current practice for creating vascular access is variable. A minimum timing from creation to use of 6 weeks has been suggested - however, the committee agreed that creation

around 6 months reflected

tree.heading: Context
